coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots-functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotubes as nanocarriers in electrochemical immunosensing. Determination of IL-13 receptor α2 in colorectal cells and tumor tissues with different metastatic potential
coredata.dc:creator: Serafín, V.
Valverde, A.
Martínez-García, G.
Martínez-Periñán, E.
Comba, F.
Garranzo-Asensio, M.
Barderas, R.
Yáñez-Sedeño, P.
Campuzano, S.
Pingarrón, J.M.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the development of the first integrated electrochemical immunosensor for the determination of the IL-13Rα2 is reported. The strategy involves the immobilization of a biotinylated capture antibody onto streptavidin-modified screen-printed electrodes through grafting with p-aminobezoic acid (p-ABA) and further activation using EDC/Sulfo-NHS chemistry. A hybrid nanomaterial composed of multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) and Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) was used as nanocarrier of multiple detector antibody and HRP molecules. Amperometric detection with the system H2O2/hydroquinone (HQ) achieved a linear calibration plot ranging from 2.7 to 100 ng mL−1 IL-13sRα2, with a LOD value of 0.8 ng mL−1. The immunosensor showed an excellent selectivity and was successfully applied to the determination of the target receptor directly in small amounts of raw cellular lysates and extracts of paraffin-embedded tissues from patients diagnosed with colorectal cancer at different stages.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
IL-13Rα2
Cancer
Electrochemical immunosensor
Paraffin-embedded tissues

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of the experimental variables involved in the preparation of the immunosensor
Application to the analysis of colon cancer cells and paraffin-embedded colorectal tissues

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic display of the different steps involved in the construction of the sandwich amperometric immunosensor for IL-13sRα2 based on the immobilization of BCAb onto Strep/p-ABA/SPCE and the use of MWCNTs/GQDs-HRP-DAb nanocarriers.

Characterization of the used nanomaterials. TEM (1–3 a) and SEM (4–6 a) images of: GQDs (1), MWCNTs (2,4), MWCNTs/GQDs (3,5) and MWCNTs/GQDs-HRP-DAb (6). UV–vis b) and Fluorescence c) spectra of acid-treated MWCNTs (black), GQDs (blue) and MWCNTs/GQDs (red). IR spectra d) of acid-treated MWCNTs (black); GQDs (blue); MWCNTs/GQDs (red) and citric acid (CA) (grey). e) Zoom of the zone delimited by a purple rectangle in Fig. 2c. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

CVs in 1.0 mM of HQ prepared in phosphate buffer solution of pH 6.0 at 50 mV s−1 recorded at: acid-treated bare SCPE (green), MWCNTs/SPCE (black), GQDs/SPCE (blue) and MWCNTs/GQDs/SPCE (red) (a). CVs recorded in 1.0 mM of HQ prepared in phosphate buffer solution of pH 6.0, containing 100 mM H2O2 at 10 mV s−1 at: GQDs/SCPE (blue), MWCNTs/SPCE (black) and MWCNTs/GQDs/SPCE (red) (b). EIS characterization of the different steps involved in the immunosensor preparation (c, d) performed in a 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− solution (prepared in 0.1 M KCl) with: bare SPCE (brown), p-ABA/SPCEs (green), activated p-ABA/SPCEs (purple), Strep/p-ABA/SPCEs (cyan blue), BCAb-Strep/p-ABA/SPCEs (black), blocked-BCAb-Strep/p-ABA/SPCEs (dark blue), IL-13sRα2-blocked-BCAb-Strep/p-ABA/SPCEs (pink) and MWCNTs/GQDs-HRP-DAb-IL13sRα2-blocked-BCAb-Strep/p-ABA/SPCEs (red). Equivalent circuits used to fit the experimental data: R1(Q1[R2W1]) (left) and R1(R2Q1)(Q2[R3W1]) (right) are shown on top of Fig. 3c and d. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

a) Calibration plots obtained for the amperometric detection of IL-13sRα2 standards with MWCNTs/GQDs-HRP-DAb-IL-13sRα2-BCAb-Strep/p-ABA/SPCEs (red) and MWCNTs-HRP-DAb-IL-13sRα2-BCAb-Strep/p-ABA/SPCEs (without GQDs) (black). Error bars are 3 times the standard deviation of three replicates. b) Real amperometric traces recorded with the MWCNTs/GQDs-HRP-DAb-IL-13sRα2-BCAb-Strep/p-ABA/SPCE immunosensors for different concentrations of IL-13sRα2 standards (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

Amperometric responses measured with MWCNTs/GQDs-HRP-DAb-IL-13sRα2-BCAb-Strep/p-ABA/SPCE immunosensors for 0 (white) and 50 ng mL−1 (grey) IL-13sRα2 standards prepared in the absence and in the presence of 10 ng mL−1 TNF, 200 ng mL−1 p53, 5 mg mL−1 BSA, 5 mg mL−1 hemoglobin, 10 ng mL−1 endoglin, 10 ng mL−1 CDH-17 and 1 ng mL−1 FGFR4. Error bars are 3 times the standard deviation of three replicates.

Assessment of IL-13Rα2 status in 20.0 μg of cell lysates and 10.0 μg of representative paired N/T tissue extracts blotted to nitrocellulose membranes and probed with the BDAb and the RhoGDi (as loading control) antibodies. Protein spots were quantified by densitometry and normalized in comparison to its corresponding loading control prior to ratio calculation.
Cyclic voltammetry in 1 mM of HQ solution prepared in 50 mM of PBS, pH 6.0, recorded at different electrode surfaces, v = 50 mV s−1.
Optimization of experimental variables affecting the analytical behavior of MWCNTs/GQDs-HRP-DAb-IL-13sRα2-BCAb-Strep/p-ABA/SPCEs immunosensors.

row.entry: Incubation time with blocking agent, min
Incubation time with IL-13sRα2 standard, min

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Concentrations used to modify the MWCNTs/GQDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Endogenous IL-13Rα2 concentration found in cell lysates and extracts from paraffin-embedded colon tissues with the developed immunosensor.

row.entry: [IL-13Rα2], ng μg−1 provided by MBs-based immunosensor developed in [13]

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots-functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotubes as nanocarriers in electrochemical immunosensing. Determination of IL-13 receptor α2 in colorectal cells and tumor tissues with different metastatic potential

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Química Analítica, Facultad de Ciencias Químicas, Universidad Complutense de Madrid, Ciudad Universitaria s/n, 28040 Madrid, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Química Analítica, Facultad de Ciencias, Universidad Autónoma de Madrid, 28049 Madrid, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Fisicoquímica, Facultad de Ciencias Químicas, Universidad Nacional de Córdoba, Argentina

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: UFIEC, Instituto de Salud Carlos III, 28220 Majadahonda, Madrid, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: IMDEA Nanoscience, Ciudad Universitaria de Cantoblanco, 28049 Madrid, Spain

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Departamento de Química Analítica, Facultad de Ciencias Químicas, Universidad Complutense de Madrid, Ciudad Universitaria s/n, 28040 Madrid, Spain.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the development of the first integrated electrochemical immunosensor for the determination of the IL-13Rα2 is reported. The strategy involves the immobilization of a biotinylated capture antibody onto streptavidin-modified screen-printed electrodes through grafting with p-aminobezoic acid (p-ABA) and further activation using EDC/Sulfo-NHS chemistry. A hybrid nanomaterial composed of multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) and Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) was used as nanocarrier of multiple detector antibody and HRP molecules. Amperometric detection with the system H2O2/hydroquinone (HQ) achieved a linear calibration plot ranging from 2.7 to 100 ng mL−1 IL-13sRα2, with a LOD value of 0.8 ng mL−1. The immunosensor showed an excellent selectivity and was successfully applied to the determination of the target receptor directly in small amounts of raw cellular lysates and extracts of paraffin-embedded tissues from patients diagnosed with colorectal cancer at different stages.

ce:section.ce:para: Many tumors show the overproduction of a range of immunosuppressive cells and cytokines [1]. The T-helper type 2 (Th2) cytokine, IL-13, is a major regulatory cytokine in various inflammatory diseases including cancer, asthma and allergy [2,3]. In most cells, IL-13 binds to an IL-13Rα1 monomer with a low affinity and then joins IL-4Rα to form a heterodimer complex which can lead to downstream pathway activation of cell apoptosis [4]. IL-13 can bind also in some normal and cancer cells to the high-affinity receptor IL-13Rα2, which was postulated as a decoy receptor [5]. Some results have demonstrated that IL-13Rα2 blocks the normal apoptotic pathway induced by IL-13/IL-4 and promotes invasion and metastasis [6]. Therefore, overexpression of IL-13Rα2, found in a variety of human cancer cells such as colorectal, glioma, squamous cell carcinoma of head and neck, and AIDS-associated Kaposi´s sarcoma [2] is considered nowadays a reliable indicator of advanced stage and aggressiveness in cancer [7].

The qualitative or semi-quantitative determination of this emerging biomarker is usually performed by flow cytometry [8], fluorescent measurements [5], Western [4] and dot blot analysis [9,10]. Quantitative measurements of this protein can be achieved using commercially available ELISA kits involving sandwich-type immunoassays using HRP-labeled detector antibodies [11]. The ELISA kits allow the determination of IL-13sRα2 within linear ranges between 30 and 100,000 pg mL−1 and with LODs ranging between 14 pg mL-1 and 0.313 ng mL−1. In addition, they were applied to the analysis of serum, plasma and cell culture media. However, they are considered quite expensive, laborious and long-lasting methodologies of applicability limited to centralized laboratories.

In this context, electrochemical immunosensors constitute an interesting alternative due to their inherent characteristics of high sensitivity and selectivity, precision and accuracy, multiplexing capability, relatively low cost, minimum sample requirement, simplicity of operation, compatibility with portable instrumentation, integration in compact analytical devices and ability to perform the determination in particularly challenging samples using simple protocols and minimal sample treatments [12–15]. Very recently our group has reported the only electrochemical immunosensor described so far for the determination of this emerging biomarker. The method involved a sandwich format implemented on the surface of carboxyl-functionalized magnetic microbeads (MBs) and amperometric transduction at screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCEs) using the HRP/H2O2/HQ system [13].

Although immunosensors involving MBs exhibit interesting properties in terms of sensitivity, assay time and matrix effect minimization, the use of MBs complicates their implementation in POCT devices and their commercialization. Conversely, integrated immune-scaffolds implemented on disposable devices provide an interesting alternative for approaching these requirements. Nowadays, the analytical performance of integrated immunosensors has been considerably improved in connection with the use of rational surface chemistries such as the diazonium salts chemistry [16], and/or smart nanomaterials integration [17] as electrode modifiers to increase the amount of immobilized bioreceptors [18], as electroactive labels [19] or as nanocarriers for loading large amounts of detector antibodies, electroactive label and/or enzyme molecules [20,21].

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [22] and oxygen-rich functional groups graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as promising nanomaterials in electrochemical immunosensing because of their outstanding physical and electrical properties [23,24]. Despite their excellent electrical conductivity and biocompatibility [25], the application of CNTs in the preparation of electrochemical immunosensors requires a previous treatment to generate enough surface anchoring groups on which biomolecules are immobilized. A common strategy is to treat CNTs with a concentrated mixture of HNO3 and H2SO4 to generate sufficient carboxylic acid groups on the side walls and tube ends [22]. On the other hand, the emerging graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanostructures are attracting increasing interest in many fields including electrochemical immunosensing [26–28], due to their unique features such as good water solubility, large surface-to-volume ratio, superior in biocompatibility and low toxicity compared to semiconducting QDs, electrochemical properties similar to graphene and abundance of hydrophilic edges and functional groups (−OH, −COOH) enhancing the loading of immunoreagents on their surface [28]. Moreover, GQDs and CNTs can easily interact through strong π–π interactions because they have similar conjugated structures [29] resulting in a hybrid nanomaterial (MWCNTs/GQDs) which merges the excellent properties of the individual nanomaterials. It is important to note that the combined use of these two nanomaterials has been reported so far just in connection with fluorescent DNA sensors [29]. Nevertheless, some interesting recent examples involving the use of CNTs combined with other materials as carrier tags in electrochemical immunosensing of relevant clinical biomarkers can be cited. Au@Pd/MoS2@MWCNTs conjugates were utilized as enzyme-mimetic labels for the amperometric detection of hepatitis B e antigen (HBeAg) in spiked serum samples [30]. Viologen-Phe-SWCNT(-HRP)-anti-TGF conjugates and magnetic multiwalled carbon nanotubes (m-MWCNTs) were used as carrier tags for the amperometric determination of TGF-β1 [31] and human fetuin A (HFA) [32] in saliva.

Considering these relevant findings, we report in this work the first integrated immunosensor for the determination of IL-13Rα2. The used strategy combines for the first time the modification of disposable electrodes through diazonium salt chemistry with the use of MWCNTs/GQDs as nanocarriers in electrochemical sensors. Screen-printed carbon electrodes were modified by grafting with p-aminobezoic acid (p-ABA). Upon activation of the surface-confined carboxylic groups via EDC/Sulfo-NHS chemistry, streptavidin was covalently immobilized, and a biotinylated specific capture antibody conjugated. A sandwich scheme was implemented using MWCNTs/GQDs-HRP-DAb conjugates as labels. The method provides a LOD of 0.8 ng mL−1 and allowed a fast and selective determination of IL-13Rα2 in raw cell lysates from human colorectal cancer cells with different metastatic potential and in small amounts of raw protein extracts from paired tumor and healthy tissues from patients diagnosed with different grade colorectal cancer.

An INBEA potentiostat provided with the Ib Graph software was used for the amperometric measurements while a μAutolab type III potentiostat (Ecochemie) controlled by FRA2 software electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was employed for other electrochemical measurements. Screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCEs, DRP-110, ϕ 4 mm) and appropriate cable connector (ref. DRP-CAC) were purchased from DropSens-Metrohm (Oviedo, Spain). A Crison model Basic 20+ pH-meter, a P-Selecta Ultrasons ultrasonic bath, a Heidolph Reax Top homogenizer for small samples and an MPW-65R centrifuge from MPW (Med. Instruments) were also employed.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded with a Cari Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer. UV visible studies were carried out with an UV–vis diode array HP 8453 spectrometer provided with HP Chemstation software. Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) was made with a JEOM 7600 electron microscope operating at 5 kV. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization was performed using a JEM 1400 PLUS microscope operating at 100 kV. A FTIR-8300 Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer was also used.

All commercial solvents and reagents used for the synthesis of (MWCNTs/GQDs) were employed without further purification. Milli-Q water was obtained from Millipore Milli-Q purification system (18.2 MΩ cm). Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs, Ø 30 ± 15 nm, 95% purity) were supplied by NanoLab, Brighton, MA. Before use, CNTs were chemically shortened and carboxylated by treatment with nitric and sulfuric acid (v/v, 1:3) under ultrasound for 5 h. The resulting product was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min, washed repeatedly with deionized water up to pH 7, and dried under nitrogen.

Other reagents used were of the highest available analytical grade. Biotin protein from Gerbu and N-(3-dimethyl-aminopropyl)-N’-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysulfo-succinimide (Sulfo-NHS), hydroquinone (HQ), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), casein, streptavidin (Strep), Tween® 20 and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (30%, w/v) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. Sodium nitrite (NaNO2) from Panreac and 4-aminobenzoic acid (p-ABA) from Across were also used. Buffer solutions were prepared in Milli-Q water. Blocker casein solutions (blocking buffer) consisted of a ready-to-use phosphate buffer solution of 1% w/v purified casein (Thermo Scientific); 10 and 100 mM phosphate buffer saline solutions of pH 7.4 (PBS) containing 137 mM NaCl, 2.7 mM KCl; 0.05 M phosphate buffer solution of pH 6.0 (PB); 0.025 M MES buffer, pH 5.0; 0.1 M sodium phosphate buffer consisting of PBS supplemented with 0.05% Tween® 20 (PBST), pH 7.4.

Recombinant human interleukin 13 soluble receptor alpha 2 (IL-13sRα2), biotinylated goat anti-human IL-13sRα2 antibody (used as capture antibody, BCAb), and mouse anti-human IL-13sRα2 antibody (used as detector antibody, DAb), all of them components of the Human IL-13sRα2 DuoSet® IC ELISA kit from R&D Systems, Inc. (Catalog Number: DY614) were used. BCAb solutions and blocking agents were prepared in 0.1 M phosphate saline buffer solution of pH 7.4, while blocking buffer was used to prepare IL-13sRα2 standard solutions.

Recombinant human TNF protein (Ref. IM1121) from Immunotech and N-terminal GST-tagged, recombinant full length human p53 protein (Catalog#14-865) from EMD Millipore Corporation, bovine serum albumin (BSA Type VH) from Gerbu Biotechnik, GmbH, human hemoglobin (H7379), human CD105 (endoglin) from Sino Biological Inc., human cadherin-17 (CDH-17) from Origene and recombinant human fibroblast growth factor receptor 4 (FGFR4), a component of the Human Total FGFR4 DuoSet® IC ELISA kit from R&D Systems Inc. (Catalog Number DYC685-2), were evaluated as potential interfering compounds.

GQDs were prepared by direct pyrolysis treatment of citric acid (CA). In a typical synthesis, 2.0 g of CA was added to a beaker and heated to 200 °C. After 5 min, the powder was gradually liquated and converted to pale-yellow color liquid, and then to orange liquid in 30 min, confirming the formation of GQDs. The obtained liquid was added dropwise into 100 mL of a freshly prepared 10 g L−1 NaOH solution under continuous stirring, generating a GQDs suspension. The synthesis of GQDs was completed by neutralizing the suspension to pH 7.0 by addition of diluted hydrochloric acid [28].
It is important to note that reproducible GQDs are synthesized by a careful control of the temperature, time and color of liquid in the citric acid pyrolysis step.

MWCNTs/GQDs composite was prepared according to the method reported by Zhang et al. [33] by dispersing 1 mg of acid-treated MWCNTs in 1.0 mL of the GQDs suspension, prepared following the protocol described in the previous section, and stirring at 1400 rpm for 30 min. Unbound GQDs were removed by successive centrifugation and the suspension was washed with Milli-Q water three times. The obtained MWCNTs/GQDs nanocomposites were finally dispersed in 1.0 mL Milli-Q water and kept in darkness at 4 °C for further use.

MWCNTs/GQDs-HRP-DAb labels were prepared following the protocol described by Akter et al. [34] by centrifugation of 500 μL of 0.1 mg mL−1 MWCNTs/GQDs suspension (14,000 rpm, 5 min) to remove the supernatant and incubating the remaining solid for 6 h at room temperature under continuous stirring and in darkness with 200 μL of a freshly prepared solution containing 400 mM EDC and 100 mM Sulfo-NHS (in 100 mM PBS pH 7.4). Thereafter, the mixed solution was centrifuged and washed with 10 mM PBS pH 7.4 three times. The resultant activated material was dispersed in 360 μL of a mixture solution containing 1.0 mg mL−1 HRP and 10 μg mL−1 of DAb (in 100 mM PBS pH 7.4) for 24 h at 4 °C. Supernatant was discarded and three washing steps with 500 μL of 10 mM PBS pH 7.4 were performed. The obtained bioconjugate nanomaterial was resuspended in 500 μL of PBST (pH 7.4) and kept in darkness at 4 °C.

20 mg of p-ABA were dissolved in 2 mL of 1 M HCl and cooled in an ice bath. The aryl diazonium salt was prepared by adding 2 mM NaNO2 aqueous solution dropwise to the above mentioned solution (38 μL for each 200 μL) under constant stirring. The SPCE was immersed in 1 mL of the resulting solution and ten successive voltammetric scans between 0.0 and -1.0 V (v = 200 mV s−1) were carried out [35]. The modified electrodes were washed thoroughly with Milli-Q water and dried at room temperature. In a second step, 10 μL of a freshly prepared EDC/Sulfo-NHS (0.1 M each in MES buffer, pH 5.0) mixture solution were placed onto the modified working electrode surface and left to react for 30 min at room temperature in a humid chamber. After rinsing with MES buffer (pH 5.0), 5 μL of a 600 μg mL−1 Strep solution (prepared also in MES buffer, pH 5.0) were placed on the working electrode surface and left to react 45 min at room temperature. The electrodes were rinsed with PBS (10 mM pH 7.4). Immobilization of BCAb was performed by adding 5 μL of a 5 μg mL−1 BCAb prepared in PBS (10 mM pH 7.4) on the Strep/p-ABA/SPCE and left to incubate 30 min at room temperature. Then, a blocking step was made by adding 10 μL of a mixture solution containing 0.5 mg mL−1 biotin and 2% (w/v) casein (prepared in 10 mM of PBS pH 7.4) and left to incubate for 90 min at room temperature. Thereafter, 5 μL of the IL-13sRα2 standard solution/sample to analyze were placed onto the electrode surface and incubated for 45 min at room temperature. After washing with 10 mM PBS, 5 μL of the MWCNTs/GQDs-HRP-DAb suspension were dropped onto the electrode and incubated for 60 min at room temperature. The resulting inmunosensor was washed again and kept with a 25 μL-drop of 10 mM PBS until the electrochemical measurement was made.

Amperometric measurements were performed by adding 45 μL of a 0.5 M PB, pH 6.0, supplemented with 1 mM HQ on the modified electrode surface and applying a potential of −200 mV vs the Ag pseudoreference electrode. Once the background current was stabilized, (˜100 s), 5 μL of a freshly prepared 50 mM H2O2 solution in 0.5 M PB pH 6.0 were added and allowed reacting for 200 s until the steady-state was reached.

Cyclic voltammetric characterization of the hybrid nanomaterial was made in 1 mM HQ solutions in 0.5 M PB, pH 6.0, in the absence (at 50 mV s−1) and in the presence of 100 mM H2O2 (at 10 mV s−1). The steps involved in the immunosensor preparation were monitored by CV and EIS in a 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− solution in 0.1 M KCl. CV measurements were made at 50 mV s−1 and EIS at the equilibrium potential of the Fe(CN)6 4−/Fe(CN)6 3− couple, with a 10 mV (rms) sinusoidal excitation amplitude and an automatic analyzer integration time (0.001% S.D. of I (jw) correlator output) with a 100 s cut-off time. Measurements were made at 10 steps per decade in the appropriate frequency range five times at each frequency and averaged during each run.

The applicability of the immunoplatform was tested for the analysis of lysates from different colorectal (KM12SM, SW480, KM12C and SW620) cancer cells, and extracts from tumour and adjacent healthy tissues from 5 patients diagnosed with different grade colorectal cancer.

SW480 and SW620 (from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC) cell repository) and KM12C and KM12SM (from I. Fidler's laboratory, MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston, TX) cell lines were grown and lysed following the protocols described previously [13–15,36]. Cell lysates were stored at −80 °C until use.

Tissue extracts from paraffin-embedded paired tumor (T) and normal (N) colorectal tissues were obtained from Hospital La Paz (Madrid, Spain). The tissues were assessed by a pathologist after written informed consent from patients and approval by the Ethical Committee of the Hospital, and obtained as previously described using three 6-μm sections of paraffin embedded tissues, placed in vials and stored at −80 °C until use [14,15,37].

Total protein concentration of the resultant cell lysates and tissue extracts were estimated using a BCA protein assay kit (Pierce, Rockford, IL) [38] and semi-quantitative analysis of IL-13Rα2 in cell lysates and tissue extracts was carried out using dot blot analysis. Briefly, 5 μg of the tissue extracts and 10 μg of cell lysates were blotted to nitrocellulose membranes. After 1 h blocking with PBS containing 0.1% Tween 20 and 3% BSA, membranes were incubated overnight at 4 °C with a 1 μg mL−1 BCAb solution followed by 1 h incubation with a 1/1,000 Strep-HRP from RayBiotech (Catalog# EL-HRP) diluted solution. Specific reactivity was visualized with SuperSignal West Pico Maximum Sensitivity Substrate (Pierce). Protein bands were semi-quantified by densitometry using Quantity One 1-D analysis software (BioRad) and normalized using RhoGDi as loading control [39].

The determination of the endogenous concentration of IL-13Rα2 in the cell lysates was carried out by direct interpolation of the signal obtained for 0.5 μg of each cell lysate into the calibration plot prepared with IL-13sRα2 standards. However, due to the strong matrix effect observed in tissue extracts, quantification in these samples was performed by applying the standard additions method by adding IL-13sRα2 in the 0–100 ng mL−1 concentration range in the presence of 0.5 μg of the corresponding tissue extract.

Fig. 1 shows schematically the different steps involved in the preparation of the MWCNTs/GQDs-HRP-DAb-IL-13sRα2-BCAb-Strep/p-ABA/SPCEs immunosensors. The SPCE surface was functionalized by electrografting of p-ABA followed by covalent immobilization of Strep. The BCAb was immobilized through streptavidin-biotin interaction and a sandwich assay configuration was implemented using MWCNTs/GQDs-HRP-DAb as nanocarriers for signal amplification. The determination of IL-13sRα2 was performed by measuring the variation in the cathodic current measured by amperometry in the presence of H2O2/HQ due to the enzymatic reduction of H2O2 mediated by HQ (see scheme reaction in Fig.1) [40,41]. H2O2 was reduced by HRP which was regenerated by HQ. The oxidized form of the mediator, benzoquinone (BQ), was reduced at the applied constant potential (-0.20 V vs. Ag pseudoreference electrode) on the electrode surface providing a cathodic current proportional to the concentration of the target protein in the sample.

Although GQDs can promote direct electron transfer, their conductivity is not good enough as they behave as semiconductors. On the other hand, it is well known that CNTs possess excellent electrical conductivity as well as good biocompatibility, high chemical stability and electrocatalytic activity [42]. Therefore, combining the properties of CNTs and GQDs into a nanohybrid material would produce a biosensing label in which CNTs act as nanowiring of electron transfer to electrode surface [43].

The characterization of the prepared hybrid MWCNTs/GQDs nanomaterial was made by different techniques. The morphology of MWCNTs/GQDs was characterized by TEM and SEM (Fig. 2 a). TEM images (1–3 in Fig. 2a) show GQDs with uniform spherical shape and average diameter of 8 ± 2 nm in agreement with previously reported results [28,44]. SEM images (4–6 in Fig. 2a) confirmed the successful preparation of the hybrid nanomaterial as well as the homogeneous modification with the biological material (image 6 in Fig. 2a).

The optical properties of synthesized MWCNTs/GQDs were characterized by fluorescence and UV–vis spectroscopy. UV–vis spectra of water soluble GQDs (Fig. 2b, blue) show a broad absorption band around 360 nm related to the π–π* transition of aromatic CC bonds (sp2 domains) [28]. The spectra of MWCNTs (Fig. 2b, black) show two adsorption bands around 230 y 330 nm related to the π–π* and n–π* transition, respectively. However, the hybrid MWCNTs/GQDs nanomaterial (Fig. 2b, red) displayed similar UV–vis absorbance bands than MWCNTs but did not exhibit the strong fluorescence of GQDs (Fig. 2c, blue), attributed to the poly-aromatic sp2 carbon nanostructures and the various surface and edge functional groups [28,45], indicating that the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between GQDs and MWCNTs took place in the nanohybrid [29]. According to Zhao et al. [46], the fluorescence intensity is strongly surface dependent and, therefore, sensitive to the structure of ligands. Indeed, when GQDs were anchored on the surface of MWCNTs the fluorescence intensity decreased by 99% (Fig. 2c, red). This dramatic quenching effect is attributed to the presence of alternative non-radiative decay pathways favoring charge transfer from the conductive band of GQDs (donor) to the empty electronic state of MWCNTs (acceptor) at the interface [47]. Charge transfer from GQDs to MWCNTs is facilitated by the intimate contact through the electrostatic interaction. This interaction was also confirmed by the slight blue-shifted emission band found for the MWCNTs/GQDs nanocomposites, with λem at 421 nm compared to 466 nm for GQDs (see Fig. 2e), in agreement with that reported by Wang et al. [48].

FTIR was used to characterize the functional groups (i.e. −OH and −COOH) of GQDs, MWCNTs, CA and MWCNTs/GQDs (Fig. 2d). GQDs spectra (Fig. 2d, blue) show the five characteristic peaks of citric acid (Fig. 2d, gray) due to CO stretching vibration at 1556 cm−1, CO stretching vibration at 1271 cm−1, −CH2 asymmetric stretching vibration at 2984 cm−1, symmetric stretching vibration at 2978 cm−1 and the OH stretching vibration from the carboxylic acid group (broad peak at 3313 cm−1) [49]. Moreover, a stretching vibration of aromatic CC bond was observed at 1374 cm−1 indicating the formation of an aromatic product from the CA base material [49].

The presence of oxygen groups such as −COOH and −OH on the acid-treated MWCNTs (Fig. 2d, black) was confirmed by the presence of several peaks including ʋOH at 3319 cm−1, ʋC=O at 1544 cm−1, ʋC–OH at 1379 cm−1, and δ OH at 1089 cm−1 [50]. The attachment of GQDs on MWCNTs by π–π* stacking interaction was demonstrated by the multiple peaks observed in the MWCNTs/GQDs spectra (Fig. 2d, red) including CO symmetric stretching vibration of the −COOH groups (1715 cm−1), CC stretching aromatic vibration (1516 cm−1), −CH2 bending vibration (1516 cm−1 and 668 cm−1) and C–O stretching vibration (1157 cm−1). Furthermore, asymmetric and symmetric −CH2 stretching bands in MWCNTs/GQDs show large red-shifts to 2921 and 2836 cm−1, respectively, compared with GQDs at 2984 and 2978 cm−1 (red and blue spectra in Fig. 2d). As previously reported, −CH2 stretching vibration was an identifying indicative of the order of alkyl chains. Thus, red-shifted bands of −CH2 stretching indicated the increased order of alkyl chains resulting from the adsorption of GQDs on MWCNTs [50,51].

Several nanomaterials such as graphene oxide [52], carbon nanodots [53], carbon nitride sheets [54] and graphene quantum dots [55] have shown to possess intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity. Similarly, the synthetized GQDs exhibit such enzymatic activity and can catalyze the non-enzymatic reduction of H2O2 mediated by hydroquinone (HQ) [49,56]. Fig. 3 a displays the CVs recorded in 1 mM HQ solution prepared in 50 mM of PBS, pH 6.0, at a bare SPCE (green curve), MWCNTs/SPCE (black curve), GQDs/SPCE (blue curve) and MWCNTs/GQDs/SPCE (red curve). Table 1 summarizes the peak current values as well as the peak-to-peak potential separation (ΔE). The MWCNTs/SPCE showed a smaller ΔE value compared to the GQDs/SPCE. Interestingly, larger peak current values and a significantly smaller ΔE were obtained with the MWCNTs/GQDs/SPCE suggesting a better electrochemical behavior of the electrode modified with the hybrid nanomaterial and confirming the synergic electrocatalytic effect achieved. The fact that the ΔE value decreased significantly only in the presence of MWCNTs indicated that this nanomaterial was responsible for the faster electron transfer observed at the modified electrode with a behaviour approaching a pseudo reversible process.

CVs were also recorded in a 1.0 mM HQ solution containing 100 mM H2O2 (Fig. 3b). The increase in the peak currents for the second scan obtained at the MWCNTs/GQDs/SPCE (ipc = 6.1 and 9.3 μA in 1st and 2nd scan, respectively) compared with the MWCNTs/SPCE (ipc = 6.5 and 4.5 μA, 1st and 2nd scan, respectively) confirmed the intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity of GQDs. These results were in agreement with previous results described in the colorimetric detection of cholesterol in the presence of Cholesterol oxidase (ChOx)/3,3´,5,5´-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) [56] and in the development of a non-enzymatic H2O2 sensor based on hybrid nanostructures of GQDs and methylene blue (MB) [49]. It is important to remark that the second cycle (solid line) exhibits a larger current than that observed in the first one scan (dotted line) only at the MWCNTs/GQDs/SPCE (CVs in red) due to the presence of GQDs immobilized on the MWCNTs. In addition, the significant decrease in the ΔE value observed at the MWCNTs/SPCE and the MWCNTs/GQDs/SPCE confirmed that the MWCNTs provoked a more rapid electrochemical kinetics.

The different steps involved in the preparation of the MWCNTs/GQDs-HRP-DAb-IL-13sRα2-BCAb-Strep/p-ABA/SPCEs immunosensors were characterized by EIS and CV using 5.0 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− solution prepared in 0.1 M KCl (Fig. 3c, d). Modification of the bare SPCE with p-ABA provoked a large increase in the electron transfer resistance, Rct, (from 797 to 16,067 Ω) due to the electrostatic repulsion between the redox probe and the negatively charged carboxylate groups. Activation with EDC/Sulfo-NHS gave rise to a dramatic decrease in the Rct value (2,157 Ω) because of the neutralization of the surface confined −COOH groups negative charge upon reaction with EDC/Sulfo-NHS [57]. As expected, further significant increase in Rct occurred after streptavidin immobilization (7,049 Ω), which is attributed to both the lower conductivity of the electrode surface coated with insulating biomolecules and the larger negative charge repulsion due to the streptavidin dissociation at the working pH value [32]. Rct value decreased again after biotinylated antibody immobilization (2,623 Ω) because of the reduction of the negative charge of streptavidin. In addition, as expected, a further decrease in the Rct value was observed after incubation with the blocking solution (mixed of casein and biotin) (342 Ω) due to the additional neutralization of the negative charge by the biotin. It is important to mention that the impedance spectra showed two different semicircles corresponding to an electrode surface on which two layers are deposited in the immunosensor preparation [58]. A similar behavior has been reported previously for other immunosensors involving incubation with casein. The equivalent circuit R1(R2Q1)(Q2[R3W1]) displayed on top of Fig. 3d corresponds to an electrode surface coated with two layers and fits best the experimental results [58–60].

A Rct slight increase (486 Ω, for the semicircle at high frequencies) was observed after IL-13sRα2 incubation due to the partially insulating barrier on the electrode surface produced by the target antigen immobilization. In addition, lower Rct value (251 Ω) was measured with the whole immunosensor (MWCNTs/GQDs-HRP-DAb-IL-13sRα2-BCAb-Strep/p-ABA/SPCEs) which may be attributed to the high conductivity provided by the MWCNTs/GQDs. CV characterization of the different immmunosensor steps is displayed in Figs. S5 a, b and the conclusions were fully consistent with the EIS results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental variables involved in the preparation of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The immunosensor preparation was designed to get suitable analytical characteristics with a simple working protocol and a short assay time. Therefore, the different variables affecting the analytical performance of the amperometric immunosensor were optimized. The tested variables were: (a) streptavidin loading and corresponding incubation time; (b) BCAb loading and incubation time; (c) blocking agent concentration and incubation time; (d) incubation time with IL-13sRα2; (e) concentration of HRP and DAb immobilized on hybrid nanomaterial; (f) incubation time with the conjugated hybrid nanomaterial. Details on these optimization studies are given in the Supporting Information (See Figs. S1–S3). The largest ratio between the currents measured with the as prepared immunosensor in the absence (N) and in the presence (S) of 100 ng mL−1 IL-13sRα2 standard, S/N ratio, was taken as the selection criterion for each tested variable. Other experimental conditions used in the development of the immunosensor such as those employed for SPCE modification by p-ABA electrografting and covalent immobilization of Strep [35], the applied potential (−0.20 V vs Ag pseudoreference electrode), the pH value [57] and the concentration of H2O2 and HQ [61] were taken from previous works. Table 2 summarizes the tested ranges and the selected values of the optimized variables.

The calibration plot constructed for IL-13sRα2 standards with the MWCNTs/GQDs-HRP-DAb-IL-13sRα2-BCAb-Strep/p-ABA/SPCE immunosensor under the optimized working conditions is displayed in Fig. 4 . A linear dependence (r2 = 0.995) of the measured cathodic current with the IL-13sRα2 concentration was found between 2.7 and 100 ng mL−1, with slope and intercept values of (6.2 ± 0.1) nA ng−1 mL and (208 ± 4) nA, respectively. Interestingly, the comparison of this calibration plot with that constructed using MWCNTs-HRP-DAb as labels, i.e. without GQDs, showed that the slope value of this latter calibration graph was more than 4 times smaller (1.41 ± 0.05) nA ng−1 mL over the same dynamic range (r2 = 0.993). This noticeable improvement in sensitivity can be attributed to the GQDs pseudo-peroxidase activity [49,56]. The LOD value achieved with the MWCNTs/GQDs-HRP-DAb-IL-13sRα2-BCAb-Strep/p-ABA/SPCE immunosensor was estimated to be 0.8 ng mL−1 (8.0 pg in 10 μL) according to the 3×sb criterion, where sb was estimated as the standard deviation (n = 10) for measurements carried out in the absence of IL-13sRα2. Moreover, a determination limit of 2.7 ng mL−1 was calculated according to the 10×sb criterion. It is important to note that the achieved LOD is slightly lower than that obtained with the only immunosensor reported to date for the determination of this emerging metastasis biomarker, using a sandwich-type immunoassay implemented on MBs (30 pg in 10 μL) [13]. Interestingly, the developed immunosensor also provides better LOD than the commercial ELISA kit which uses the same immunoreagents (8.0 vs 31.3 pg IL-13sRα2) and allows the determination to be made in a remarkably shorter time (1 h 45 min vs 4 h 40 min, once the BCAb-Strep/p-ABA/SPCEs and CAb-plate were prepared and blocked, respectively). In addition, the immunosensor works with portable and cost-effective instrumentation which makes it more attractive for routine determinations in decentralized settings and implementation in POCT devices.

The reproducibility of the amperometric measurements was tested by measuring the current for 50 ng mL−1 IL-13sRα2 with six different immunosensors prepared in the same manner and the same day. A relative standard deviation (RSD) value of 5.9% was calculated indicating the good reproducibility of the protocols used for the immunosensor preparation and the amperometric transduction. On the other hand, the storage stability of BCAb-Strep/p-ABA/SPCEs (after the blocking step) was tested by preparing the same day various immunosensors which were stored at 4 °C in a humid chamber and used in a daily control to measure 0 and 100 ng mL−1 IL-13sRα2 standard solutions. The obtained results (Fig. S6a) revealed that the initial immunosensor response was kept within the limits of control, set at ± 3 times the standard deviation of the measurements (n = 3) carried out the first day of the study (preparation day), for the next 15 days. Moreover, the stability of the MWCNTs/GQDs-HRP-DAb conjugates was also checked by preparing and keeping them in the refrigerator at 4 °C under constant stirring. The amperometric responses of the corresponding immunosensors for 0 and 100 ng mL−1 IL-13sRα2 standard solutions (Fig. S6b) did not show significant differences for at least 30 days after the MWCNTs/GQDs-HRP-DAb conjugates preparation. This storage stability is better or similar than that reported for other CNTs conjugates such as multi-HRP-flake like Fe3O4-coated CNTs nanocomposites (HRP-CNTs@f-Fe3O4-Ab2, 15 days, [62]) or m-MWCNTs(-HRP)-Anti-HFA (30 days [32],) used as carrier tags in electrochemical immunosensors.

The selectivity of the developed immunosensor was tested by comparing the current values measured for 0.0 and 50.0 ng mL−1 IL-13sRα2 standards prepared in the absence and in the presence of various non-target compounds commonly found in serum at the concentration levels expected in this type of biological sample. Fig. 5 shows that, despite the high concentrations of some potential interfering compounds in particular BSA and hemoglobin, no significant effect was apparent on the determination of IL-13sRα2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to the analysis of colon cancer cells and paraffin-embedded colorectal tissues
ce:section.ce:para: The developed immunosensor was applied to the determination of the target IL-13Rα2 protein in raw lysates of colon cancer cells with different metastatic potential and in paired tumor (T) and healthy (N) tissues from patients diagnosed with colorectal cancer at different stages. Importantly, quantification of the target protein receptor in these complex biological samples could be accomplished by using just 0.5 μg of the corresponding cell lysate/tissue extract.

No apparent matrix effect was observed for cell lysates. For instance, the slope value for the linear calibration graph recorded in SW480 lysates was (6.2 ± 0.2) nA ng−1 mL. Therefore, the determination in cell lysates was performed by interpolation of the amperometric responses measured in each lysate into the calibration plot constructed with IL-13sRα2 standards (slope value of (6.2 ± 0.1) nA ng−1 mL). However, a noticeable matrix effect was observed in the tissues extracts. The slope value constructed in the T tissue number 1 was, for instance (3.7 ± 0.4) nA ng−1 mL. Accordingly, the determination of the target protein in tissue extracts was carried out by extrapolation of calibration plots performed for IL-13sRα2 standards (in the 0–100 ng mL−1 concentration range) prepared in the presence of 0.5 μg of the tissue extract analyzed.

The results obtained for three replicates of each sample are summarized in Table 3 . As it can be observed, IL-13Rα2 was overexpressed in metastatic colon cancer cells (SW620 and KM12SM) in comparison with their respective isogenic non-metastatic colon cancer cells (SW480 and KM12C), in agreement with that reported in the literature [4]. Moreover, the concentrations calculated in the cells lysates are also in agreement with those reported for the same cells using a MBs-based immunosensor [13] (see comparative results in Table 3).

Regarding the analysis of paraffin-embedded tumor tissues, larger IL-13Rα2 contents were found in extracts from colorectal cancer tissues compared to healthy tissues. It is worth to remark that the data given in this paper are the first quantitative data reported so far to measure the expression of this protein receptor in paraffin-embedded tumor tissues.

The obtained data were compared with semi-quantitative results of IL-13Rα2 expression provided by dot blot analysis in both types of samples. Fig. 6 shows an acceptable correlation between both types of results. However, it is important to note that the immunosensor is able to provide quantitative results in less than 2 h and with only 0.5 μg of extract, while the dot blot semi-quantitative method required 10–20 μg of extract and more than 14 h for the assay.

This work reports the first integrated immunosensor for the determination of the IL-13Rα2 biomarker. The approach combines the use of disposable electrodes functionalized by diazonium salt chemistry with a hybrid nanomaterial composed of MWCNTs and GQDs employed as advanced nanocarriers of multiple enzyme and detector antibodies to achieve signal amplification. The immunosensor design involves the use of bio-scaffolds prepared by BCAb immobilization onto Strep-modified electrografted SPCEs further activated through EDC/Sulfo-NHS chemistry. The immunosensor exhibits attractive analytical characteristics in terms of selectivity and sensitivity providing a LOD value 4-times lower than that reported for the only electrochemical immunosensor described so far for the determination of the same target protein receptor, as well as for the commercial ELISA kit using the same immunoreagents which, in addition, requires twice test time and involves instrumentation of limited applicability to centralized laboratories. Comparative results with immunosensors prepared with MWCNTs/GQDs and MWCNTs bioconjugates confirmed the important improvement in sensitivity achieved with the former design due to the intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity of GQDs. The integrated immunosensor was successfully applied to the determination of IL-13Rα2 in less than 2 h and in a small amount (0.5 μg) of cells lysates and extracts from paraffin-embedded tumor tissues. The method involves a simple protocol and portable and cost-effective instrumentation, features which make it advantageous compared with ELISA and dot-blot methodologies for the development of automated POC systems for on-site determination of this metastatic cancer-related biomarker even in challenging samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support of the Spanish Ministerio de Economía y Competitividad, Grants CTQ2015-70023-R and CTQ2015- 64402-C2-1-R, and the NANOAVANSENS Program from the Comunidad de Madrid (Grant S2013/MT-3029), are gratefully acknowledged. R.B. acknowledges the financial support of the PI17CIII/00045 Grant from the AES-ISCIII program. M.G.-A. was supported by a contract of the Programa Operativo de Empleo Juvenil y la Iniciativa de Empleo Juvenil (YEI) with the participation of the Consejería de Educación, Juventud y Deporte de la Comunidad de Madrid y del Fondo Social Europeo.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.01.012.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Elevated myeloid-derived suppressor cells in pancreatic, esophageal and gastric cancer are an independent prognostic factor and are associated with significant elevation of the Th2 cytokine interleukin-13
Role of interleukin-13 in cancer, pulmonary fibrosis, and other TH2-type diseases
Targeting of IL-4 and IL-13 receptors for cancer therapy
High expression of IL-13 Receptor α2 in colorectal cancer is associated with invasion, liver metastasis, and poor prognosis

IL-13 regulates cancer invasion and metastasis through IL-13Ralpha2 via ERK/AP-1 pathway in mouse model of human ovarian cancer
Glioma IL13Ralpha2 is associated with mesenchymal signature gene expression and poor patient prognosis

IL-13 receptor α2 selectivity inhibits IL-13 induced responses in the murine lung
IL-13 receptor α2 signaling requires a scaffold protein, FAM120A, to activate the FAK and PI3K pathways in colon cancer metastasis

Determination of cadherin-17 in tumor tissues of different metastatic grade using a single incubation-step amperometric immunosensor
Disposable amperometric immunosensor for the determination of the E-cadherin tumor suppressor protein in cancer cells and human tissues
Integrated affinity biosensing platforms on screen-printed electrodes electrografted with diazonium salts
Sensitive electrochemical immunosensor for α-synuclein based on dual signal amplification using PAMAM dendrimer-encapsulated Au and enhanced gold nanoparticle labels
Sensitive detection of proteins using difunctional DNA–gold nanoparticles
Sensitive immunosensor for cancer biomarker based on dual signal amplification strategy of graphene sheets and multi-enzyme functionalized carbon nanospheres
Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications
Current progress on the chemical modification of carbon nanotubes

Increased electrocatalyzed performance through dendrimer-encapsulated gold nanoparticles and carbon nanotube-assisted multiple bienzymatic labels: highly sensitive electrochemical immunosensor for protein detection
Electroreduction based electrochemical enzymatic redox cycling for the detection of cancer antigen 15-3 using graphene oxide modified indium−tin oxide electrodes
Electrochemical immunosensor with graphene quantum dots and apoferritin-encapsulated Cu nanoparticles double-assisted signal amplification for detection of avian leukosis virus subgroup
A novel label-free electrochemical immunosensor based on functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for carcinoembryonic antigen detection
Ultrasensitive determination of receptor tyrosine kinase with a label free electrochemical immunosensor using graphene quantum dots modified screen-printed electrodes
DNA nanosensor based on biocompatible graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes
Ultrasensitive electrochemical immunosensor for quantitative detection of HBeAg using Au@Pd/MoS2@MWCNTs nanocomposite as enzyme-mimetic labels
Viologen-functionalized single-walled carbon nanotubes as carrier nanotags for electrochemical immunosensing. Application to TGF-β1 cytokine
Magnetic multiwalled carbon nanotubes as nanocarrier tags for sensitive determination of fetuin in saliva
Detection of α-fetoprotein with an ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence paper device based on green-luminescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Ultrasensitive nanoimmunosensor by coupling non-covalent functionalized graphene oxide platform and numerous ferritin labels on carbon nanotubes
Ultrasensitive detection of adrenocorticotropin hormone (ACTH) using disposable phenylboronic-modified electrochemical immunosensors
Electrochemical sensor for rapid determination of fibroblast growth factor receptor 4 in raw cancer cell lysates
An optimized predictor panel for colorectal cancer diagnosis based on the combination of tumor-associated antigens obtained from protein and phage microarray

A proteomic analysis reveals that snail regulates the expression of the nuclear orphan receptor nuclear receptor subfamily 2 group F member 6 (Nr2f6) and interleukin 17 (IL-17) to inhibit adipocyte differentiation
Identification of prefrontal cortex protein alterations in Alzheimer’s disease
3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine as electrochemical substrate for horseradish peroxidase based enzyme immunoassays. A comparative study
Amperometric biosensor for hydrogen peroxide, using supramolecularly immobilized horseradish peroxidase on the β‐cyclodextrin‐coated gold electrode
Effect of carbon nanotubes on direct electron transfer and electrocatalytic activity of immobilized glucose oxidase
Electrochemistry and electroanalytical applications of carbon nanotubes: a review
Graphene quantum dot modified screen printed immunosensor for the determination of parathion
Synergistically enhanced activity of graphene quantum dot/multi-walled carbon nanotube composites as metal-free catalysts for oxygen reduction reaction
Effect of different types of surface ligands on the structure and optical property of water-soluble PbS quantum dots encapsulated by amphiphilic polymers
Controlled assembly of CdSe/MWNT hybrid material and its fast photoresponse with wavelength selectivity
Tuning the charge-transfer property of PbS-quantum dot/TiO2-nanobelt nanohybrids via quantum confinement
Non-enzymatic hydrogen peroxide sensor based on graphene quantum dots-chitosan/methylene blue hybrid nanostructures
A layer-nanostructured assembly of PbS quantum dot/multiwalled carbon nanotube for a high-performance photoswitch
Colorimetric platform for visual detection of cancer biomarker based on intrinsic peroxidase activity of graphene oxide
B-doped carbon quantum dots as a sensitive fluorescence probe for hydrogen peroxide and glucose detection
Graphite-like carbon nitrides as peroxidase mimetics and their applications to glucose detection
In situ growth of silver nanoparticles on graphene quantum dots for ultrasensitive colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose
Colorimetric detection of cholesterol based on highly efficient peroxidase mimetic activity of graphene quantum dots, Sensor Actuat
Disposable and integrated amperometric immunosensor for direct determination of sulfonamide antibiotics in milk
An electrochemical immunosensor for brain natriuretic peptide prepared with screen-printed carbon electrodes nanostructured with gold nanoparticles grafted through aryl diazonium salt chemistry
An electrochemical impedance sensor for the label-free ultrasensitive detection of interleukin-6 antigen
A short footnote: circuit design for faradaic impedimetric sensors and biosensors
An electrochemical immunosensor for testosterone using functionalized magnetic beads and screen-printed carbon electrodes
Sensitive electrochemical immunoassay for chlorpyrifos by using flake-like Fe3O4 modified carbon nanotubes as the enhanced multienzyme label

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Veronica Serafín received her degree in Chemistry (Master in Science and Chemical Technology) at the Complutense University of Madrid in 2011. She defended her European PhD in Analytical Chemistry at the Universidad Complutense de Madrid (Spain) in 2015. She spent four months of her PhD at the University of Teramo, Italy. She worked as junior researcher at the IK4-CIDETEC Technological Centre during 2015. Currently, she is a postdoctoral researcher contracted by an article 83 with the company Mirnas Biosens S.L. Her work focuses in the preparation of immuno- and nucleic acid-based biosensors based on nanostructured electrochemical platforms for determination of clinical biomarkers.

Alejandro Valverde obtained the Degree in Chemistry in 2016 and the Master Degree in Chemical Sciences and Technologies in 2017 from the Complutense University of Madrid. Since 2017 he is working on his PhD in the Analytical Chemistry Department at the Complutense University of Madrid as member of the research group of “Electroanalysis and Electrochemical Biosensors”. His research focuses on the development of electrochemical biosensors for the determination of cancer-related and emerging diseases biomarkers for clinical diagnosis.

Gonzalo Martínez-García received his BSc Degree in Chemistry and Msc Degree in Science and Chemical Technology at the Complutense University of Madrid in 2012. He defended his International PhD in Advanced Analytical Chemistry at the same University in 2018. He spent four month of his PhD at the University of Coimbra, Portugal. Actually he is working at Orion S.L. R&D Company. His scientific activity focuses on the development of electrochemical enzymatic and immunosensing nanostructured platforms for the determination of clinical biomarkers.

Emiliano Martínez-Periñán received his bachelor’s degree and Master degree in chemistry from Universidad de Cádiz. He obtained his PhD degree from Universidad Autónoma de Madrid in 2016. After that he had been working as a visiting postdoc researcher at Manchester Metropolitan University under the direction of professor Craig E. Banks. Then, he obtained a Juan de la Cierva-Formación fellowship from the Spanish Ministry of economy and innovation at the Electroanalysis and Electrochemical Biosensors group of Universidad Complutense de Madrid. Nowadays he is working as associated professor in the department of Analytical Chemistry and Instrumental analysis of Universidad Autónoma de Madrid. His research interests include electrosynthesis, nanotechnology, the use of different analysis techniques coupled with electrochemistry and electrochemical sensors.

Fausto Nahuel Comba obtained his degree of Biochemist (2007) and a PhD in Chemistry (2014) from the National University of Córdoba, Argentina. He did the postdoctoral training in the Physical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Chemical Sciences (Córdoba National University). At present, he is assistant professor at Department of Chemical Engineering, Regional School of Villa Maria of the National Technological University and Assistant Researcher at Argentine Research Council (CONICET). His research interests cover bioelectrochemistry, chemical biosensors, biosensing applications and university technological entrepreneurship.

María Garranzo Asensio received her Bachelor’s Degree in Biochemistry from the Universidad Complutense de Madrid (Spain) in 2013. In 2015, she obtained her Master’s Degree in Neuroscience at the Universidad Autónoma de Madrid (Spain). She is currently working on her PhD in Biochemistry, Molecular Biology and Biomedicine at the Functional Proteomics laboratory in the Instituto de Salud Carlos III and in collaboration with the Universidad Complutense de Madrid. Her research interests include the use of proteomic techniques to find potential disease biomarkers that could help establish new diagnostic signatures.

Rodrigo Barderas received his PhD in Biochemistry and Molecular Biology from the Complutense University of Madrid (Spain) in 2004. Since 2004 he has been working in the field of proteomics. Since 2017 he is currently the Head of the Functional Proteomics Unit in the Instituto de Salud Carlos III at the Chronic Disease Programme. His areas of interest include the identification of diagnostic, prognostic markers and new targets of intervention by means of proteomics in chronic diseases, with a special focus on colorectal cancer. He has authored over 80 research papers and book chapters.

Paloma Yáñez-Sedeño obtained his pH.D. (1983) from Complutense University of Madrid. In 1988 she gained a position of Assistant Professor in the Faculty of Chemistry of the University Complutense, and occupied the Academic Secretary at the Department of Analytical Chemistry from 1988 to 1992. Since 2008, she is a full Professor of Analytical Chemistry at the Complutense University of Madrid. Her research interests focus on electroanalysis, nanostructured electrodes, enzyme biosensors and immunosensors. She has over 180 publications including peer-reviewed papers, book chapters and text books. She has conducted fourteen Research Projects dedicated to the determination of additives, drugs and toxic compounds in food, and monitoring of doping substances, low molecular-weight hormones, and marker proteins of obesity, ageing, and related diseases. She is currently Treasurer of the Spanish Royal Society of Chemistry.

Susana Campuzano received her PhD in analytical chemistry from the Universidad Complutense de Madrid (Spain) in 2004. Since 2005 she works as Assistant Professor at the Analytical Chemistry Department of the Chemistry Faculty of the Universidad Complutense de Madrid where she is member of the “Electroanalysis and Electrochemical (Bio)sensors” research group headed by Prof. J.M. Pingarrón. She worked as a Research Scholar in the research group of Prof. J. Wang at the Department of Nanoengineering in UCSD (USA) from January 2010 to July 2011. Her areas of interest include the development of enzymatic, immuno and DNA electrochemical sensors and advanced nanomachines. She has authored over 170 research papers and several book chapters.

José M. Pingarrón obtained his PhD in Sciences from Complutense University of Madrid (1981). He is Professor of Analytical Chemistry at Complutense University of Madrid and head of the group “Electroanalysis and electrochemical (bio)sensors”. Current research includes the development of nanostructured electrochemical biosensors, including enzyme electrodes, immunosensors and genosensors for the ultrasensitive detection of bacteria, low molecule weight hormones and cancer markers as well as electrochemical arrays for multiplexed detection. He is Associate Editor of the journal Electroanalysis and Vice-president of the Spanish Royal Society of Chemistry and since 2017 Fellow of the International Society of Electrochemistry. He is author or co-author of over 400 peer-reviewed papers, 30 book chapters and 2 text books as well as 7 invention patents.
